title: Communication Path Integrity — Unexpected Route or ASN Changes

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-012-1 R3 Part 3.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-012-1 R3 Part 3.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.63: Communication Path Integrity — Unexpected Route or ASN Changes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.63' running on index=netflow sourcetype=flow:internet or sourcetype=thousandeyes:tests, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=netflow sourcetype=flow:internet cip012_path=1 earliest=-24h
| stats values(dest_asn) as asn_list by src_cc, dest_cc, hour
| lookup cip012_approved_asn.csv src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn OUTPUT approved
| mvexpand asn_list
| rename asn_list as dest_asn
| lookup cip012_approved_asn.csv src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn OUTPUT approved
| where isnull(approved)
| table src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn, hour
description: We detect when BGP or path monitoring shows control center interconnects traversing unexpected autonomous systems or countries.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes netflow and sourcetypes flow:internet, flow:internet`, thousandeyes:tests`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build approved ASN matrix with carriers. (2) Enrich flows with GeoIP cautiously. (3) Alert NOC on drift. (4) Document maintenance reroutes in lookup. Indexes required: `index=netflow`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=flow:internet`, `sourcetype=flow:internet``, `sourcetype=thousandeyes:tests``.
visualization: Table (unexpected ASNs), Map (optional), Timeline. Key fields: `asn_list`, `dest_asn`, `dest_cc`, `hour`, `src_cc`.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, registered change tickets, and approved work can look like noncompliance in raw telemetry; confirm against the current change record before you escalate.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=netflow` `sourcetype=flow:internet` or `sourcetype=thousandeyes:tests`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Build approved ASN matrix with carriers. (2) Enrich flows with GeoIP cautiously. (3) Alert NOC on drift. (4) Document maintenance reroutes in lookup.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=netflow sourcetype=flow:internet cip012_path=1 earliest=-24h
| stats values(dest_asn) as asn_list by src_cc, dest_cc, hour
| lookup cip012_approved_asn.csv src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn OUTPUT approved
| mvexpand asn_list
| rename asn_list as dest_asn
| lookup cip012_approved_asn.csv src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn OUTPUT approved
| where isnull(approved)
| table src_cc, dest_cc, dest_asn, hour
```

Understanding this SPL

**Communication Path Integrity — Unexpected Route or ASN Changes (CIP-012-1 R3 Part 3.1)** — We detect when BGP or path monitoring shows control center interconnects traversing unexpected autonomous systems or countries.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=netflow` `sourcetype=flow:internet` or `sourcetype=thousandeyes:tests`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: netflow; **sourcetype**: flow:internet. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=netflow, sourcetype=flow:internet, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` aggregates events by **src_cc, dest_cc, hour**, producing one row per unique combination for trend analysis and threshold evaluation.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Expands multivalue fields with `mvexpand` — use `limit=` to cap row explosion.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(approved)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
Output fields: asn_list, dest_asn, dest_cc, hour, src_cc.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP firewall log, Windows Security, or the asset register—whichever the search is built to mirror. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unexpected ASNs), Map (optional), Timeline
grandmaExplanation: We detect when BGP or path monitoring shows control center interconnects traversing unexpected autonomous systems or countries. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
